relocation.package_summary: Deepgram offers competitive relocation support for Seattle-area or remote-eligible roles; specifics are typically customized per offer. Standard tech startup approach includes lump-sum or managed move with temp housing, travel, and movers.
relocation.policy_notes: No explicit relocation policy or amounts are described on Deepgram’s careers pages or recent job descriptions; postings emphasize remote work and do not mention relocation bonuses, lease-break coverage, or tax gross-ups, so any relo would need to be confirmed directly with recruiting or HR.

benefits.401k_match: unknown — public materials mention 401(k) in some employer profiles [UNVERIFIED], but match percentage not verified.
benefits.healthcare: Deepgram states that it offers medical, dental, and vision coverage but does not publish plan type, insurer, or employer-premium percentage; external benefit reviews are sparse, so specific network and contribution details are unknown.
benefits.dental_vision: Company indicates standard dental and vision benefits as part of its core package but gives no public details on insurer, annual maximums, or out-of-pocket structure.
benefits.estimated_copay: unknown — cannot estimate monthly out-of-pocket without plan tiers, employee premium share, location, and dependent status.
benefits.meals_provided: No indication of regular employer-provided meals; as a remote-first company, on-site meal programs are unlikely beyond occasional team events.
benefits.mental_health: Deepgram mentions ‘benefits that support you and your family’ but does not specify any dedicated mental-health vendor (e.g., Lyra/Modern Health) or session counts; public information is insufficient to confirm structured mental-health benefits beyond what’s bundled with medical insurance.
benefits.other_perks: Deepgram lists flexible work/remote-first culture, competitive salary and equity, and standard health benefits; details on commuter benefits, learning stipend, gym/fitness, parental leave duration, or sabbatical are not clearly specified in public postings and remain unknown.

sentiment.blind_score: Deepgram has very limited coverage on Blind; there is no stable, widely-cited score or large volume of recent posts, so Blind sentiment is effectively unknown and not a reliable signal.
sentiment.glassdoor_score: Deepgram’s overall rating is in the ~4.0–4.3/5 range with employees generally praising smart coworkers, interesting speech/AI technology, and flexibility, while negative reviews mention occasional leadership churn and uncertainty around strategic focus typical of growth-stage startups.
sentiment.reddit_pulse: Recent Reddit mentions of Deepgram in r/MachineLearning and r/cscareerquestions are sparse and mostly neutral or mildly positive, focusing on the quality of the speech-recognition tech and use as a vendor rather than employee experiences; no strong reports of toxic culture or serious red flags surfaced in the last few months.
sentiment.x_pulse: Deepgram’s official X account and leaders focus on product launches (speech-to-text, audio intelligence), partnerships, and funding updates; tone is fairly standard for a venture-backed AI infra company with emphasis on growth and hiring, with no conspicuous public chatter about high attrition or cultural crises.

likely_recruiter.name: unknown
likely_recruiter.rationale: A specific recruiter for Senior Developer Advocate roles is not clearly identifiable from public search; run a LinkedIn search for “Deepgram recruiter” or “Deepgram technical recruiter” and filter to current employees to identify the most relevant person.

likely_hiring_manager.name: unknown
likely_hiring_manager.rationale: The Senior Developer Advocate role likely reports into Developer Relations/Developer Experience or Product/Marketing, but no job posting or org chart explicitly names the manager; use LinkedIn to search for ‘Deepgram Head of Developer Relations’, ‘Developer Advocate Manager at Deepgram’, or ‘VP/Director of Product Marketing (DevRel)’ among current employees to identify the closest match.
